Patch Set 2:

I can't be sure, but I suspect this patch might just be papering over a deeper problem.

If calling dput() results in a final call to iput(), then the reference to the inode through page->mapping->host cannot be safe to use ... which is surprising.

So I doubt this patch really fixes the problem, but I don't know what the real problem is.

Patch Set 2:

> I can't be sure, but I suspect this patch might just be papering
 > over a deeper problem.
 > 
 > If calling dput() results in a final call to iput(), then the
 > reference to the inode through page->mapping->host cannot be safe
 > to use ... which is surprising.

huh? why is it not safe to use if we have a reference?

the problem is final dput potentially blocks on network IO and that's a bit no-no in context of the thread that is responsible in eagerly consuming said network io

Patch Set 2:

> the problem is final dput potentially blocks on network IO

From the stack trace, it is really the final iput() which blocks - and that final iput() follow from the final dput().
But my question is: why is there any chance of a final iput() happening here.  We hold a reference to the page, and the page holds a reference to the inode, so how can a final iput() happen?

Patch Set 2:

> > the problem is final dput potentially blocks on network IO
 > 
 > From the stack trace, it is really the final iput() which blocks -
 > and that final iput() follow from the final dput().
 > But my question is: why is there any chance of a final iput()
 > happening here.  We hold a reference to the page, and the page
 > holds a reference to the inode, so how can a final iput() happen?

 > > the problem is final dput potentially blocks on network IO
 > 
 > From the stack trace, it is really the final iput() which blocks -
 > and that final iput() follow from the final dput().
 > But my question is: why is there any chance of a final iput()
 > happening here.  We hold a reference to the page, and the page
 > holds a reference to the inode, so how can a final iput() happen?

Hm, but cannot truncate happen even on pages that are referenced? That would remove page->mapping and drops the reference on the inode then? The page itself is still there and des not go anywhere, just it's no longer part of the inode (hence the mapping removal)

Patch Set 2:

Yes, a page that is referenced can be truncated.  Then truncate_inode_page() will call delete_from_page_cache() and the page->mapping will be set to NULL.
This requires having a lock on the page.  If you don't have a lock on the page, then you cannot trust ->mapping.

Is ll_dirty_page_discard_warn() called with a lock on the page.
If it is, then it shouldn't be possible for the bug to happen.
If it is not, them it shouldn't be doing anything with page->mapping->host.

As it only wants the inode for an error message, I would suggest trylock_page().  If that succeeds, then get the inode, find a dentry and use "%pD4" to print out the dentry.
If the trylock fails, or the inode is NULL, or the dentry cannot be found, just print a generic message.

Patch Set 2:

So, I was going to add additional refcounting for inode use in vvp layer, and to pass the node from vvp_vmpage_error to ll_dirty_page_discard_warn() and then I noticed this comment in the vvp_vmpage_error:

 * This takes inode as a separate argument, because inode on which error is to
 * be set can be different from \a vmpage inode in case of direct-io.

and this got me somewhat confused - how can we have two different inodes referring to the same file in memory at the same time in case of direct io? Is this comment incorrect? It dates all the way back to 2008 when clio code was introduced before t underwent a bunch of changes

Patch Set 2:

so I guess to answer my own question. The inode in page in case of directio might be different because directio page is some userspace pointer that I am not even sure might have an inode. It's certainly not the one we want to print anyway, the inode we want to print is the one where IO happens, so we should be passing that inode into the ll_dirty_page_discard_warn function.

Now to get to the inode, this should alleviate the the issue of incorrect/unsafe page->mapping-> use.

Now the "unreferenced" inode from the vvp is actually sorta reference because it's attached to the vvp and when inode is torn down vvp is freed, if we reference inode from vvp then inode refcount never drops and inodes are never freed causing assertion failures on cleanup in lustre as we try to free various other caches.

So I think if we just amend this patch with passing inode to ll_dirty_page_discard_warn() it should be enough to fix the problem
Uploaded patch set 3.

Oleg and I discussed this - One *very* minor thing is that the page actually can't from DIO, this is the mechanism for returning *some* error when an async (ie, buffered) i/o fails.  (The comment in the code is wrong.)

So if this is refreshed it would be nice to correct that and the commit message

parents.subject: LU-15282 lod: less spinlock on the alloc rr

commit.subject: LU-15340 llite: Delay dput in ll_dirty_page_discard_warn
commit.message:
LU-15340 llite: Delay dput in ll_dirty_page_discard_warn

Otherwise we can be final dput and need to wait for pages
to clear which is bad because this is called from ptlrpcd
that is not supposed to block esp. for network traffic as
it can cause livelocks if it happens to be needed to kill
the very same RPC we are waiting on.

Additionally pass in the inode from IO since the page
we are using might come from directio and that is
probably not even a valid inode.

Fixes: 624a3ac23393 ("LU-921 llite: warning in case of discarding dirty pages")
Change-Id: Ie2f1a34047145202c11a4e1a0b18b2e01d9e4601
